sections.title: Use
items: Actions or states that took place before a specific point in the past: By the time the guests arrived, we **had already set** the table. She **had worked** at the company for ten years before she became manager.
Past actions whose results were relevant at a particular moment in the past: The players were celebrating because they **had won** the match.

sections.title: Useful notes
rows: I'd finished my homework **by** eight o'clock.
**By the time** I got to class, the lesson had started.
The teacher had checked the answers **before** the lesson.
I left **after** I'd finished the test.
Simon had **just** finished the test when the bell rang.
I left **when** I'd finished the test.

sections.title: Be careful
items: Choosing between the past simple and the past perfect simple changes the order of events.
The show started when we arrived. (= We arrived first, then the show started.)
The show had started when we arrived. (= The show started first, then we arrived.)
Some verbs have irregular past participle forms.

sections.title: Use
items: Activities that were in progress up to, or until just before, a moment in the past: He was out of breath because he **had been running**. They deserved a rest — they **had been working** all morning.

sections.title: Useful notes
rows: Tony had been studying **for** hours, so he had a headache.
She'd been hoping to win the competition **since** the summer.
We'd been talking about the Internet **before** the lesson started.

sections.title: Be careful
items: The past perfect simple often emphasises the result of an action: ✓ She'd **written** an article for the school newspaper. (= She'd finished it.)

The past perfect continuous often emphasises the action, and the time spent on the action, rather than the result: ✓ She'd **been writing** an article for the newspaper. (= She'd started, but she hadn't finished it.)

exercises.title: A: Past perfect simple
exercises.instructions: Complete using the correct past perfect simple form of the verbs in brackets.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete using the correct past perfect simple form of the verbs in brackets.

items.sentence: When we got to the station, the train ...... (already / leave).
items.explanation: The train left **before** we arrived → past perfect **had already left**.

items.sentence: Mia ...... (finish) her homework before dinner.
items.explanation: Homework finished **before** dinner → **had finished**.

items.sentence: They ...... (not / book) a table, so the restaurant was full.
items.explanation: No table booked **before** they arrived → **hadn't booked**.

items.sentence: ...... (Tom / ever / fly) before he went to Canada?
items.explanation: Experience **before** Canada → **Had Tom ever flown**.

items.sentence: The match started after ...... (the referee / check) the pitch.
items.explanation: Referee checked **before** the match started → **the referee had checked**.

items.sentence: I couldn't buy a ticket because ...... (I / leave) my wallet at home.
items.explanation: Wallet left at home **before** trying to buy → **I had left**.

items.sentence: ...... (you / already / finish) lunch when I arrived?
items.explanation: Lunch finished **before** I arrived → **Had you already finished**.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Choose the sentence with the same meaning
exercises.title: B: Choose the sentence with the same meaning
exercises.instructions: Choose the option that means the same.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the option that means the same.

items.context: We had cleaned the kitchen when Mum came home.
items.sentenceBefore: We had cleaned the kitchen when Mum came home.
options: Mum came home and then we cleaned the kitchen.
We cleaned the kitchen and then Mum came home.
items.originalSentence: We had cleaned the kitchen when Mum came home.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "B" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "B" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "Mum came home and then we cleaned the kitchen." would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.Mum came home and then we cleaned the ki: "Mum came home and then we cleaned the kitchen." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.context: I called Sam after I had finished my project.
items.sentenceBefore: I called Sam after I had finished my project.
options: I finished my project and then I called Sam.
I called Sam and then I finished my project.
items.originalSentence: I called Sam after I had finished my project.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "A" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "I finished my project and then I called Sam." would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.I finished my project and then I called: "I finished my project and then I called Sam." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.context: By the time the film started, we'd bought popcorn.
items.sentenceBefore: By the time the film started, we'd bought popcorn.
options: We bought popcorn before the film started.
We bought popcorn after the film started.
items.originalSentence: By the time the film started, we'd bought popcorn.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "A" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "We bought popcorn before the film started." would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.We bought popcorn before the film starte: "We bought popcorn before the film started." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.context: Lily didn't leave until her brother had arrived.
items.sentenceBefore: Lily didn't leave until her brother had arrived.
options: Lily left before her brother arrived.
Her brother arrived and then Lily left.
items.originalSentence: Lily didn't leave until her brother had arrived.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "B" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "B" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "Lily left before her brother arrived." would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

"Lily left before her brother arrived." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.context: The teacher hadn't marked the tests by Friday.
items.sentenceBefore: The teacher hadn't marked the tests by Friday.
options: The tests were still unmarked on Friday.
The teacher marked the tests before Friday.
items.originalSentence: The teacher hadn't marked the tests by Friday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "A" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "The tests were still unmarked on Friday." would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

"The tests were still unmarked on Friday." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.context: They had sold the bike before I phoned about it.
items.sentenceBefore: They had sold the bike before I phoned about it.
options: They sold the bike and later I phoned.
I phoned and then they sold the bike.
items.originalSentence: They had sold the bike before I phoned about it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "A" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "They sold the bike and later I phoned." would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

"They sold the bike and later I phoned." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.context: Dad had cooked dinner when we arrived.
items.sentenceBefore: Dad had cooked dinner when we arrived.
options: We arrived and then Dad cooked dinner.
Dad cooked dinner and then we arrived.
items.originalSentence: Dad had cooked dinner when we arrived.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "B" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "B" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "We arrived and then Dad cooked dinner." would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

"We arrived and then Dad cooked dinner." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Write sentences using the prompts
exercises.title: C: Write sentences using the prompts
exercises.instructions: Write sentences using the prompts. One of the verbs must be in the past perfect simple.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write sentences using the prompts. One of the verbs must be in the past perfect simple.

items.displayPrompt: they / already / leave / when / we / arrive
items.answer: They had already left when we arrived.
acceptedAnswers: They had already left when we arrived.
items.explanation: They left **before** we arrived → **had already left when we arrived**.

items.displayPrompt: I / not / meet / Anna / before / she / join / our class
items.answer: I hadn't met Anna before she joined our class.
acceptedAnswers: I hadn't met Anna before she joined our class.
items.explanation: Meeting Anna happened **before** she joined → **hadn't met**.

items.displayPrompt: when / Dad / get home / we / already / eat / dinner
items.answer: When Dad got home, we had already eaten dinner.
acceptedAnswers: When Dad got home, we had already eaten dinner.
items.explanation: Dinner eaten **before** Dad got home → **had already eaten**.

items.displayPrompt: he / be / tired / because / he / not / sleep / well
items.answer: He was tired because he hadn't slept well.
acceptedAnswers: He was tired because he hadn't slept well.
items.explanation: Bad sleep **before** feeling tired → **hadn't slept well**.

items.displayPrompt: by the time / the concert / begin / everyone / find / their seats
items.answer: By the time the concert began, everyone had found their seats.
acceptedAnswers: By the time the concert began, everyone had found their seats.
items.explanation: Seats found **before** the concert began → **had found their seats**.

exercises.title: D: Past perfect continuous
exercises.instructions: Look at the pictures and complete the sentences. Use the correct form of the past perfect continuous.
exercises.studentInstruction: Look at the pictures and complete the sentences. Use the correct form of the past perfect continuous.

items.sentence: He was covered in paint because ...... (paint / the fence).
items.answer: he had been painting the fence
acceptedAnswers: he had been painting the fence
items.explanation: Paint on him shows ongoing activity before now → **had been painting the fence**.

items.sentence: They were exhausted because ...... (walk / all morning).
items.answer: they had been walking all morning
acceptedAnswers: they had been walking all morning
items.explanation: Exhaustion from walking all morning → **had been walking all morning**.

items.sentence: The streets were wet because ...... (it / rain).
items.explanation: Wet streets show rain before → **it had been raining**.

items.sentence: Did she lose her voice because ...... (sing / for hours)?
items.answer: she had been singing for hours
acceptedAnswers: she had been singing for hours
items.explanation: Singing for hours caused the lost voice → **had been singing for hours**.

items.sentence: When the bus came, ...... (we / wait / for forty minutes).
items.answer: we had been waiting for forty minutes
acceptedAnswers: we had been waiting for forty minutes
items.explanation: Waiting started before the bus came → **we had been waiting for forty minutes**.

items.sentence: When I called, ...... (they / not / study / long).
items.explanation: They hadn't studied long **before** the call → **hadn't been studying long**.

items.sentenceAfter: for ten minutes when the phone rang.
items.explanation: Only ten minutes of sleep before the phone rang → **been sleeping** (duration).
Tickets bought **before** you called → past perfect simple **bought**.

items.sentenceAfter: us about the change, so we were surprised.
items.explanation: She hadn't told us **before** we were surprised → **told** (completed earlier).

items.sentenceAfter: football for an hour before it started raining.
items.explanation: An hour of playing **before** rain → **been playing** (continuous duration).

items.sentenceAfter: his room all morning, so it looked much better.
items.explanation: Tidying all morning (ongoing) → **been tidying**.

items.sentenceBefore: It was my first time abroad because I'd never
items.explanation: Never been abroad **before** this trip → **been** (past perfect of **be**).

exercises.title: F: Find the extra word
exercises.instructions: If a line is correct, click OK. If there is an extra word in a line, click on it.
exercises.studentInstruction: If the line is correct, tap OK. If there is an extra word, tap it.

items.sentence: Yesterday our team finally [had] won the school basketball final.
items.explanation: **Yesterday** needs past simple **won** — **had** is the extra word.

items.sentence: We had [been] trained hard for weeks, so everyone was excited.
items.explanation: **Had been trained** is wrong here; **been** is the extra word — say **had trained**.

items.sentence: Before the game started, the coach [had] gave us a short talk.
items.explanation: After **had**, use past participle **given**, not past simple **gave** — **had** is wrong.

items.sentence: The other team scored first, but we didn't give up.
items.explanation: This sentence is correct — no extra word to remove.

items.sentence: By half-time, we had [been] scored three points.
items.explanation: **Been** doesn't fit before **scored** — remove **been** (say **had scored**).

items.sentence: I was tired because I had been running up and down the court all morning.
items.explanation: This sentence is correct — **had been running** is the right past perfect continuous.

items.sentence: When the referee blew the final whistle, everyone shouted with joy.
items.explanation: This sentence is correct — no extra word.

items.sentence: Our captain had prepared a speech before the match.
items.explanation: This sentence is correct — **had prepared** is fine.

items.sentence: [Been] After we had collected our medals, we took photos.
items.explanation: **Been** at the start is the extra word — remove it.

items.sentence: By the time we got home, Dad had [yet] told all our neighbours!
items.explanation: **Yet** doesn't fit after **had** here — remove **yet**.
